However, Abu alahi is known as albei is one of the great companions and scholars of the prophet sallall.aii wasallam's disciples. He narrates to us that the messenger of Allah sallall.aii said, he says, "The virtue that a scholar holds over a aid or someone who is very dutiful. Arabid is someone who has kind of devoted himself and devoted the mass bulk of his or her time to the worship of Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala. So he's referred to as what? as aid is someone who is very devout, very dutiful in his worship of Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala from amongst the general population of the Muslim. He says the virtue that a scholar holds over aid is the virtue that I hold over the least amongst you. He's speaking about himself. He's saying that the virtue that the scholar holds over thee dutiful dutiful in the worship of Allah devout but is not a person of knowledge as a layman a lay Muslim but he is or she is someone who has devoted the majority of his or her life to the worship of Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala constantly in fasting or remembrance of Allah or or salah or zaka or jihad. This is the prophet sallall.aii wasallam says to the companions the virtue that a scholar holds over the most dutiful is the virtue that I hold over the least amongst you. the most obscure unknown companion who maybe just interacted with the prophet sallall.aii wasallam once in his life because again what is a companion? A companion is anyone who saw the prophet sallall.aii wasallam as a believer and died upon Islam. That's what a companion is. So the bare minimum is that a person be Muslim and see the prophet sallall.aii wasallam even if he doesn't speak to him. This is now a companion as long as he dies upon that belief. So he's saying the virtue that a scholar holds over is the virtue that I hold over the least amongst you. sallallahu alaihi wasallam. And in another wording of this same narration in the prophet Sam says he said the virtue that a scholar holds over aid is the virtue that the moon holds over the rest of all the stars in the sky. meaning how much more pronounced is the moon? How much more necessary, how much more dire is the moon to our existence and our experience here on this earth. So much more than our need for the rest of the stars. We need all of these celestial bodies. We need it all. They're there for a reason. But the moon cannot be done without. If one of the stars in the sky went out or 10 or 20 or 100 or 200, maybe perhaps certain people wouldn't notice. The majority of humanity may not notice. However, if the if the moon didn't rise tomorrow, if it disappeared, what would happen to humanity? Everything would change. Even the deepest part of the ocean would change. and the most obscure part of the earth would change because of the absence of the moon. And so the prophet sallall.aii wasallam says the virtue that a scholar holds over the rest of the devout is the virtue that the moon holds over the rest of the stars in the sky. The virtue that a scholar holds over the generally devout is the virtue that I hold over the least amongst you. He then says He then follows this up with saying, "Indeed, Allah and his angels and the inhabitants of the heavens and the earth, even an ant in its hole, and a fish in the sea, all are making a salah upon those who teach the people good." making a salah upon them. A salah. What is this from the people? What is in this context? Salah from the rest from humanity is a dua. But it's not just any dua. It's not just any supplication. A salah. When I say for instance or you say Allah, oh Allah, may your salah be upon whoever the prophet sallall.aii wasallam. It is a dua that Allah raise that person's esteem. So when you sayham, you are essentially saying, "Oh Allah, I ask that you raise the esteem and mention of Muhammad in the highest of levels and in the highest of settings. That is what a salah is." So the prophet sallallah.aii alaihi wasallam. A scholar is someone who has learned immensely and is deeply rooted in knowledge of the Quran, the Sunnah, and the companions. Well, if I don't know any better than my heart is in the right place and you know I just didn't know but my heart is in the right place, I'll be okay because Allah knows what's in my heart. If your heart was in the right place, you would learn what Allah wants from you. If you haven't learned what Allah wants from you, your heart is not in the right place. No matter if you don't double park or take your garbage out every Tuesday and Thursday or whatever else you figure is makes you a good person, a good person is only the one who is good in the scales of Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala. And the whole concept of, well, at least I'm not as bad as that guy over there. Trust me, on the day of judgment, that guy over there will not factor in to your judgment in front of Allah in any way. When you get a job, do you just march in, put your feet up on the desk, and do whatever you and say, "Hey, hey, hey, hey, my heart's in the right place. Relax." Is that what you say? But I did not intend to break the law. My heart was in the right place. Has any of us prepared for that? Not one of us. We would assume that person is either disingenuous or something's wrong upstairs. Maybe the elevator isn't going all the way to the penthouse kind of thing. That is not an argument in any reasonable setting. So why has that become our argument in front of Allah? All of them. All of them. summary: This lecture discusses a hadith narrated by Abu al-Darda about the virtue of scholars over the devout worshippers (al-'abidin). The speaker explains that a scholar's superiority is compared to the Prophet Muhammad's superiority over the least of his companions, and in another narration, to the moon's superiority over the stars. The lecture emphasizes that scholars, teachers of good knowledge, receive prayers (salah) from Allah, His angels, and all creation. The second part focuses on the importance of seeking Islamic knowledge, arguing that ignorance is a fatal disease in the modern age of information, and that Muslims have a responsibility to learn Islam properly from qualified scholars rather than relying on good intentions alone.
suggested_title: The Virtue of Scholars and the Importance of Seeking Islamic Knowledge
catchy_title: Why scholars matter more than you think
